guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic — Acute cystitis in non-pregnant women (eTG)
MBS 585-600 (urgent after-hours attendance — cited in source note)
intentional_test_features: Calibration test — MINOR/COMMON condition must be tiered LOW: an acute lower urinary tract infection is telehealth/GP/pharmacist manageable (empiric first-line antibiotic + safety-net), NOT an emergency. The source note frames it as an 'urgent after-hours home visit' (in-person clinician billing context); the AI Doctor must NOT over-triage to ED/000. A correct answer is recognise + telehealth antibiotic access + pyelonephritis safety-net.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person home visit (clinician palpated the suprapubic area, checked CVA tenderness, measured vitals, ran an on-site urine dipstick). None of these are available to a telehealth AI Doctor — the AI reasons from patient-reported symptoms + patient-observable urine change and cannot confirm the diagnosis remotely.

Errors-of-commission probe (pharmacology): the source co-prescribes a urinary alkaliniser WITH nitrofurantoin — alkaline urine reduces nitrofurantoin efficacy. This is a flagged interaction the AI Doctor should recognise.
Age and sex not stated in source — inferred (adult female) and flagged.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source case CIA-045.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
